paragraphs.text: To whom it may concern, I am writing in support of the creation of stricter consumer protection rules for online interactions under the Digital Fairness Act proposed here. I commend the Commission for the continuous support of consumer rights with legislation such as the DSA and DMA and would like to express my support for continued strict enforcement of these (e. g. I'd like to see more pressure on Apple to properly open their devices to third-party apps with no strings attached). As a frequent user of digital products, I strongly support this proposal for further consumer protections. Like the proposal describes there are several areas where large tech corporations continue to exploit users with anti-consumer practices and these should be constrained. Dark patterns like the infamously extremely obtuse Amazon Prime cancellation "Odyssey" disenfranchise end users and especially those who are less savy or abled or more vulnerable to psychologic tricks like this. Introducing rules like a "one-click-cancellation" policy would be a great improvement and should be a goal of this DFA. Similarly, I would strongly welcome better tools to manage subscriptions or how my data is used for personalisation as called out in the further points of the proposal, as well as an end to highly personalised and quickly changing pricing on online storefronts. Stricter rules for advertising, not only for influencers, are also always a good thing. On the topic of products like video games, the proposal calls out practices that exploit vulnerable users such as minors, I would like to note that the also described "addictive design features" are a well-documented feature in this space. Practices like lootboxes and microtransactions are often designed specifically to make users spend more than intended, not least by obfuscating the real cost behind alternative in-product currencies. These practices prey not just on minors but also people susceptible to gambling or addiction problems and therefore should be regulated more strictly to reduce unwanted spend and psychological issues. Specifically on the topic of video games, I would like to direct the Commission's attention to the recently concluded European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" with over 1.4 million signatures pre-verification. Video games are an artform that is only accessible digitally and as such the nowadays all too common practice of publishers making their games unavailable after a certain time despite often very feasible technical requirements to prevent this represents a considerable and preventable loss of cultural heritage of not only European citizens, but the entire planet's, especially in the nascent digital culture. Furthermore, copies to a game are typically sold, not rented or provided as a service, therefore current practice may run afoul of existing laws of ownership. In the interest of digital fairness, I thus urge the Commission to consider the points of the ECI and include better protections against such digital obsolescence in the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you for your consideration.
